coredata.dc:title: Development and validation of a graphene quantum dot-based sensor for abacavir quantification via fluorescence quenching
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Alqahtani, Ali
Alqahtani, Taha
Al Fatease, Adel
Almrasy, Ahmed A.
coredata.dc:description: Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) is a global health concern, and the development of effective methods for the detection and quantification of antiretroviral drugs is crucial for monitoring therapeutic efficacy and patient compliance. Herein, we present a novel approach utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the determination of abacavir, a widely used nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor in the treatment of HIV. The sensing mechanism was investigated through Stern-Volmer analysis, thermodynamics studies, and density functional theory calculations, which revealed the strong binding interactions between GQDs and abacavir via a static quenching process. Factors affecting the analytical performance, such as pH, GQDs concentration, and incubation time, were systematically optimized to achieve a linear detection range of 100–1000 ng/mL with a low detection limit of 17.49 ng/mL. The method was validated in accordance with ICH guidelines demonstrating excellent linearity, accuracy, precision, robustness and selectivity making it suitable for the quantification of abacavir in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples. The method also demonstrated higher sensitivity and more environmentally friendly characteristics when compared to previously reported chromatographic techniques, showcasing the potential of GQDs as a superior alternative for the traditional detection approaches of pharmaceutical compounds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Abacavir
GQDs
Spectrofluorimetry
Greenness assessment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GQDs and spectral characteristics

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by Deanship of Research and Graduate Studies at King Khalid University, Saudi Arabia, Project No. (RGP2/399/45).
The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Research and Graduate Studies at King Khalid University for funding this work through Large Research Project under grant number RGP2/399/45.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of the abacavir functionalized GQDs showing a potent emission at a λ max of 453 nm when excited at 350 nm. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra resulting from the quenching process of the functionalized GQDs with various abacavir concentrations.

(A) Stern-Volmer plots showing the relationship between F0/F and abacavir concentrations at different temperatures (298 K, 303 K, and 313 K). The decreasing slope with increasing temperature suggests a static quenching mechanism. (B) Van ‘t Hoff plot showing the temperature dependence of the binding constant (Ka).

(A) Mulliken charge distribution calculated using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with a 3-21G basis set. Color scale ranges from green (positive charge) to red (negative charge), illustrating the partial charge on each atom. (B) Electrostatic potential (ESP) surface derived from the same DFT calculations, visualizing the charge distribution across the molecular surface. This analysis reveals key electronic features of abacavir, including charge localization on heteroatoms and the resulting electrostatic potential landscape, which are crucial for understanding its interactions with GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Investigation of experimental factors impacting the stability of fluorescence quenching of GQDs by abacavir, such as (A) pH of the buffer, (B) buffer volume, (C) quantity of the GQDs, and (D) duration of incubation. The error bars illustrate the standard error derived from three independent measurements.

Greenness assessment of (A) the developed method and (B) the reported chromatographic method using the AGREE tool to determine their environmental impact. Blueness assessment of (C) the developed method and (D) the reported chromatographic method using the BAGI tool to determine their analytical practicability.
Validation data for determination of abacavir by the developed functionalized GQDs fluorescent probe.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured on the same day.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: % RSD of 9 determinations (3 concentrations repeated 3 times) measured in the three consecutive days.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Quantitative analysis of abacavir in the commercial Ziagen ® tablets by the proposed method and statistical comparison with the reported method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values in parenthesis are tabulated values of “t “and “F” at (P = 0.05).

head.ce:title: Development and validation of a graphene quantum dot-based sensor for abacavir quantification via fluorescence quenching

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology, College of Pharmacy, King Khalid University, Abha, 62529, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacology, College of Pharmacy, King Khalid University, Abha, 62529, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Khalid University, Abha, 62529, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Cairo, 11751, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Cairo 11751, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) is a global health concern, and the development of effective methods for the detection and quantification of antiretroviral drugs is crucial for monitoring therapeutic efficacy and patient compliance. Herein, we present a novel approach utilizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the determination of abacavir, a widely used nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor in the treatment of HIV. The sensing mechanism was investigated through Stern-Volmer analysis, thermodynamics studies, and density functional theory calculations, which revealed the strong binding interactions between GQDs and abacavir via a static quenching process. Factors affecting the analytical performance, such as pH, GQDs concentration, and incubation time, were systematically optimized to achieve a linear detection range of 100–1000 ng/mL with a low detection limit of 17.49 ng/mL. The method was validated in accordance with ICH guidelines demonstrating excellent linearity, accuracy, precision, robustness and selectivity making it suitable for the quantification of abacavir in pharmaceutical formulations and biological samples. The method also demonstrated higher sensitivity and more environmentally friendly characteristics when compared to previously reported chromatographic techniques, showcasing the potential of GQDs as a superior alternative for the traditional detection approaches of pharmaceutical compounds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) is a life-threatening condition that has affected millions of people worldwide, particularly in resource-limited settings and poor countries [1]. This virus attacks the body's immune system, specifically CD4 T cells, reducing its ability to fight off infections and diseases [2]. If left untreated, HIV can progress to acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS), a stage where the immune system is severely compromised [3]. As a result, effective treatment regimens that ensure viral suppression are crucial in managing HIV, and recent advancements in antiretroviral therapies have significantly improved patient outcomes and quality of life [4]. While newer antiretroviral medications are commonly prescribed in developed nations, abacavir remains an important component of HIV treatment regimens in resource-limited settings [5]. As a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor, abacavir requires prior HLA-B∗5701 genetic screening to identify patients at risk of hypersensitivity reactions, highlighting the importance of personalized medicine in HIV treatment [6]. For patients who test negative for HLA-B∗5701, accurate and sensitive quantification of abacavir is essential for therapeutic monitoring and optimizing treatment strategies. Current analytical methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC-UV) [7,8] and liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [9,10] have been explored for the determination of abacavir in pharmaceutical formulations and biological matrices but these approaches often require complex sample preparation, lengthy analysis times, and expensive instrumentation. Electrochemical methods such as differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV) methods have been reported for the detection of abacavir [11], but these techniques may suffer from matrix interferences and low sensitivity. UV spectrophotometry has also been used for the quantification of abacavir particularly in combination with lamivudine and dolutegravir [12,13], but this approach can lack the required selectivity and sensitivity for accurate determination in complex biological samples. Hence, there is a pressing need for the development of novel sensing platforms that can provide enhanced sensitivity and selectivity for abacavir detection with suitability for point-of-care in resource-limited settings.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a promising class of nanomaterials for analytical applications due to their unique optical and electronic properties, high surface area, and excellent biocompatibility [14–17]. For example, their photoluminescent properties have been leveraged to detect various analytes, even in complex matrices [18–21]. Furthermore, their biocompatibility and straightforward synthesis make GQDs an attractive candidate for constructing greener and environmentally friendly fluorescent sensors for pharmaceutical and biomedical applications [22,23]. Another advantage of using GQDs is their optical properties that can be tuned by modifying the structure and composition, allowing for the rational design of fluorescent sensors with desired selectivity and sensitivity [24–26]. In this context, the employment of fluorescent methods based on GQDs could provide a viable strategy for the sensitive and selective determination of abacavir with much more sensitivity, selectivity and cost-effectiveness compared to traditional analytical techniques.

This study presents the first application of GQDs for detecting the antiretroviral drug abacavir. The synthesized GQDs have been characterized using various analytical techniques. The sensing mechanism is investigated through comprehensive analyses including Stern-Volmer plots, density functional theory calculations, and thermodynamic studies to elucidate the GQD-abacavir interactions. The experimental conditions are systematically optimized to maximize analytical performance, and the sensor is validated according to ICH guidelines to ensure reliability in pharmaceutical and biological samples. Additionally, the method's environmental impact and analytical practicality are evaluated against existing techniques using analytical greenness (AGREE) [27] and blue applicability grade index (BAGI) [28] assessment tools, demonstrating its advantages over traditional approaches.

GQDs (1–4 nm diameter, >99 % purity), acetonitrile (HPLC grade, ≥99.9 %), and sodium tetraphenylborate (ACS reagent, ≥99.5 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Abacavir sulphate (reference standard, purity 99.3 %) was obtained from the Egyptian Drug Authority (EDA), Cairo, Egypt. The pharmaceutical formulation analyzed was Ziagen® (300 mg abacavir film-coated tablets) manufactured by ViiV Healthcare, Inc., UK. Buffer components were analytical grade reagents obtained from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemical Company (Cairo, Egypt) and prepared according to USP guidelines. HPLC-grade distilled water was used throughout all experiments.

Functionalized GQDs were prepared as follow: Briefly, 50 mL of 10 −2 M sodium tetraphenylborate solution was mixed with 50 mL of 10 −2 M abacavir sulphate solution and the resulting precipitate was allowed to stand for 12 h at room temperature. The precipitate was then collected by filtration, washed with distilled water, and dried under vacuum. 100 mg of the resulting solid was mixed was 200 mg of GQDs and the mixture was dissolved in 70 mL water and sonicated for 1 h. The volume was then completed to 100 mL with water to obtain an aqueous dispersion of abacavir-functionalized GQDs (3 mg/mL). The prepared abacavir-GQDs were comprehensively characterized to evaluate their physicochemical properties. The morphological and structural properties were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), and zeta potential measurements. Surface chemistry and functional groups were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), while the optical properties were assessed through spectrofluorimetric measurements and quantum yield determination.

Fluorescence measurements were carried out using a JASCO FP-6200 spectrofluorometer equipped with 150 W Xenon arc lamp as the excitation source and 1 cm quartz cells. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were determined by performing a series of screening experiments which resulted in λ ex = 350 nm and λ em = 453 nm. The monochromators slit width were set at 5 nm for both excitation and emission. Spectral acquisition and data analysis were performed using the Spectra Manager software. UV measurements were conducted using a Shimadzu-1800 double-beam spectrophotometer. pH measurements were carried out using a Jenway 3510 pH meter (Jenway, UK). A JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope operated at 200 kV was employed to obtain the morphological information of the GQDs. FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet iS5 FT-IR spectrometer in the wavenumber range of 4000-400 cm−1 using the KBr pellet technique. The hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of the prepared GQDs were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS.
A stock solution of abacavir sulphate (100 μg/mL) was prepared in water, and further dilutions were made to obtain a working standard solution of 10 μg/mL.

The optimal experimental parameters for the fluorescent detection of abacavir using the abacavir-functionalized GQDs were investigated by studying the effect of solution pH, the amount of GQDs, and incubation time. In 10-mL volumetric flasks, 1 mL of the functionalized GQD solution was mixed with 1 mL borate buffer solution (pH 8.0) and various volumes of the abacavir standard solution. The solutions were diluted to the mark with distilled water and mixed thoroughly to achieve final concentrations of 100–1000 ng/mL then allowed to stand at room temperature for 5 min. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity was measured using the predefined excitation and emission wavelengths (F). A blank experiment was also performed under the same conditions without adding the abacavir standard solution(F0). Then, a calibration curve was constructed by plotting the fluorescence quenching ratios (F0/F) vs the abacavir concentration, and the linear regression equation was calculated.

The developed method was validated according to ICH guidelines for parameters such as linearity, limit of detection, limit of quantification, accuracy, precision, robustness and selectivity. Linearity was determined using 7 calibrator points covering 100–1000 ng/mL working range. The correlation coefficient (R2) was calculated to assess the linearity of the method, ensuring that the response of the fluorescence quenching was proportional to the concentration of abacavir, which is essential for reliable quantification in complex biological matrices. The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were determined based on the standard deviation of the residuals and the slope of the calibration curve.

Accuracy and precision were evaluated by analyzing quality control samples at three different concentrations in triplicate (250, 500 and 750 ng/mL) in the same day and in three different days to determine the within-day and between-day variations, respectively. Robustness was assessed by varying the experimental conditions, such as slight changes in pH, GQD concentration and reaction time, to evaluate the method's stability under different circumstances, thus ensuring its reliability in practical applications. Selectivity of the method was investigated by analyzing placebo samples containing the common pharmaceutical excipients and checking for any interference with the abacavir signal as well as testing the method for the simultaneous determination of abacavir in the presence of other antiretroviral drugs frequently co-administered.

Finally, the developed method was applied for the quantitative analysis of abacavir in commercial pharmaceutical tablets. Five film coated tablets were weighed and ground to a fine powder in a mortar. An amount equivalent to 10 mg of abacavir was accurately weighed, transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask, dissolved in 50 mL water, sonicated for 15 min and diluted to the mark to obtain a solution labeled to contain 100 μg/mL of abacavir. The solution was then filtered through a 0.45 μm filter to remove any undissolved particles, and the concentration of abacavir was determined using the established fluorescent probe method.

The method was also applied to the determination of abacavir in spiked human plasma samples to evaluate its applicability in biological matrices. The spiked plasma samples were prepared by adding known amounts of abacavir to blank plasma, followed by the extraction process, which includes 1 mL acetonitrile to precipitate protein and the mixture was vortexed for 5 min. After centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant was collected, evaporated to dryness, and the residue was reconstituted in water for subsequent fluorescence analysis using the developed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and spectral characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: The comprehensive characterization of the functionalized GQDs was performed using multiple complementary techniques. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis revealed a narrow size distribution with an average particle size of 3.11 ± 0.63 nm (Fig. S1A), indicating excellent monodispersity. This observation was further corroborated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging, which showed well-dispersed, spherical GQDs with uniform morphology and average size of 3.20 nm (Fig. S1B). The colloidal stability of the GQDs was confirmed through zeta potential measurements, which demonstrated a negative surface charge of −20.1 mV (Fig. S1C), suggesting sufficient electrostatic repulsion to prevent aggregation in aqueous media.

The surface chemistry of the GQDs was investigated using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. S1D). The spectrum exhibited several characteristic bands: a broad absorption band around 3400 cm⁻1 corresponding to O–H stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups on the GQD surface. The peaks in the region of 2800–3000 cm⁻1 can be attributed to C–H stretching vibrations. The prominent band at approximately 1650 cm⁻1 suggests CO stretching of carboxyl groups, while the peaks in the 1400–1600 cm⁻1 region correspond to CC stretching vibrations of the aromatic carbon network. The presence of these oxygen-containing functional groups on the GQD surface not only contributes to their water dispersibility but also provides potential binding sites for interactions with abacavir molecules.

The photoluminescent properties of the GQDs were evaluated by determining their quantum yield (QY) using quinine sulphate as a reference standard. Under optimal conditions, the GQDs exhibited a quantum yield of 25.75 %, which is notably high for carbon-based quantum dots. This substantial quantum yield indicates efficient radiative recombination processes within the GQDs and confirms their excellent fluorescent properties, making them suitable for sensitive fluorescence-based detection applications. Furthermore, the spectral characteristics of the GQDs were evaluated using spectrofluorimetric analysis. The excitation and emission wavelengths were determined to be 350 nm and 453 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 A). The emission spectrum exhibited a significant fluorescence intensity, which was further analyzed to explore the quenching efficiency of abacavir on the GQDs, allowing for a deeper understanding of the interaction dynamics between the drug and the nanomaterial used in the probe development. Upon addition of abacavir to the GQD solution, a concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching was observed (Fig. 1B), suggesting the ability of the drug to interact with the GQDs and affect their optical properties, an essential aspect for the development of a sensitive fluorescent probe.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of the abacavir-GQD system was investigated using Stern-Volmer analysis (Fig. 2 ). Generally, there are two types of fluorescence quenching: dynamic quenching, which is due to the collision between the fluorophore and the quencher, and static quenching, which is due to the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex between the fluorophore and the quencher. A temperature dependent study was performed to distinguish between these two quenching mechanisms. For this purpose, the fluorescence intensity of the GQD solution was recorded at various temperatures, revealing that as the temperature increased, the quenching efficiency of abacavir decreased (Fig. 2A), indicating a predominance of static quenching in the observed system, with Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) values of 5.29 × 10 5, 4.72 × 10 5, and 4.24 × 10 5 M−1 at 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K, respectively (Table S1). This behavior supports the formation of a stable ground-state complex that reduces the excited-state lifetimes of the GQDs in the presence of abacavir, highlighting its potential as a sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for this drug in biological applications. Additionally, the association constant (Ka) was determined from the Stern-Volmer analysis and found to be 2.66 × 10 5 M−1 at 298 K, indicating a strong interaction between abacavir and the GQD nanomaterial. It is worth mentioning that the inner filter effect was excluded as a possible quenching mechanism since no absorbance was observed for abacavir at the excitation and emission wavelengths of the GQDs (Fig. S2).

Several thermodynamic parameters have been also calculated as raveled from the Van't Hoff plot (Fig. 2B), including the enthalpy change (ΔH°) and the entropy change (ΔS°) of the abacavir-GQD binding process, which were found to be −36.11 kJ/mol and −17.27 J/(mol.K), respectively indicating that the interaction between abacavir and the GQDs is spontaneous and mainly driven by hydrogen bonding and Van der Waals interactions (Table S1). Gibb's free energy change of −30.9 kJ/mol further confirmed the favorable nature of the binding process between abacavir and the GQD nanomaterial (Table S1). DFT calculations was then applied to gain further insights into the abacavir electronic properties and its possible binding mode with the GQD surface. A basis set of 3-21G was used together with the B3LYP functional, to accurately model the molecular orbitals and assess the electronic properties of abacavir. Mulkin population analysis revealed that the amino groups of abacavir carry a significant positive charge (Fig. 3 A), which enables the formation of hydrogen bonding interactions with the oxygen-containing functional groups on the GQD surface, thus explaining the observed static quenching mechanism. This was also confirmed using the electrostatic potential map of abacavir which revealed a localized high electron density around the amino groups, facilitating the hydrogen bonding with the GQDs (Fig. 3B).

To further improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the abacavir fluorescent sensor, several experimental parameters were optimized, including the pH of the medium, buffer volume, GQDs concentration, and incubation time (Fig. 4 ). Among these parameters, the pH of the medium was found to significantly influence the quenching efficiency, with optimal fluorescence intensity observed at pH 7–9 (Fig. 4A), which in agreement with previous reports [19] and aid in the ionization of the functionalized GQDs at slightly basic conditions. Decreasing the pH to acidic range resulted in severe fluorescence quenching, potentially due to the protonation of the GQD surface and the disruption of the binding interaction with abacavir. Moreover, the buffer volume was optimized to enhance the interaction between abacavir and the GQDs, with the optimal condition established at 1 mL of borate buffer, pH 8 (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, the concentration of GQDs was adjusted to maximize sensitivity, demonstrating peak quenching at a concentration of 300 μg/mL (Fig. 4C), while incubation time of 5 min was found to ensure equilibrium between the GQDs and abacavir (Fig. 4D). The combination of these optimized conditions resulted in a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent probe for the determination of abacavir in different matrices.

The linearity range of 100–1000 ng/mL was established through systematic evaluation of the GQD-based sensor response. At concentrations below 100 ng/mL, the sensor exhibited non-linear behavior, while concentrations above 1000 ng/mL resulted in saturation of the GQD fluorescence quenching. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the sensor demonstrated excellent linearity within this range, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9997 and a low residual standard deviation of 0.0095, confirming the robust linear relationship between fluorescence response and abacavir concentration. The method achieved a limit of detection (LOD) of 17.49 ng/mL and limit of quantification (LOQ) of 53.00 ng/mL (Table 1 ), demonstrating sufficient sensitivity for trace-level determination of abacavir in complex samples. Additionally, the precision and accuracy of the method were evaluated through intra-day and inter-day assays, yielding relative standard deviations of less than 2 % and average recovery of 100.65 ± 0.752 (n = 3), which are consistent with the ICH guidelines (Table 1).

Robustness of the developed method was tested by slight variations in the critical parameters such as pH, GQD concentration, and incubation time, and the results remained unaffected, demonstrating the reliability of the proposed fluorescent probe for the determination of abacavir (Table 1). Furthermore, selectivity studies were conducted by evaluating the potential interference from common pharmaceutical excipients, ionic species and pooled plasma samples, and no significant interference was observed, confirming the high specificity of the GQD-based sensor for abacavir detection (Fig. S3). Besides, common co-administered drugs with abacavir in HIV therapies, such as lamivudine, zidovudine, and dolutegravir, did not affect the fluorescence quenching (Fig. S4), indicating the ability of the developed method to selectively quantify abacavir even with other antiretroviral compounds present.

The applicability of the proposed GQD-based fluorescent assay was assessed by determining the abacavir content in commercial pharmaceutical formulations and spiked human plasma samples. The results demonstrated average recovery of 99.45 ± 1.628 (n = 5), for pharmaceutical preparations validating the method's effectiveness for quantifying abacavir in pharmaceutical preparation which were consistent with reported HPLC-UV method [7]. Statistical analysis in terms of mean and variances showed no significant difference between the two quantification approaches, confirming the validity and accuracy of the developed fluorescent sensor (Table 2 ).

Spiked plasma samples were also analyzed using the GQD-based method, and the recovery rates were found to be within the acceptable range of 95.25–106.68 %, with CV% below 8 % highlighting the ability of the developed sensor to accurately quantify abacavir in complex biological matrices without the need for extensive sample pretreatment (Table S2). However, bio analytical validation with additional real patient samples would be required to further establish the practical utility of the proposed method for therapeutic drug monitoring and pharmacokinetic studies of abacavir in clinical settings.

The developed GQD-based fluorescent sensor for quantifying abacavir demonstrates several advantages over the conventional analytical techniques, such as HPLC and LC-MS, in terms of greenness and sustainability. The use of eco-friendly GQDs as the recognition element and the absence of organic solvents or derivatization steps minimizes the generation of chemical waste, making the proposed method more environmentally benign. Moreover, the rapid and simple procedure reduces time and resource consumption, further contributing to its sustainability profile. Besides, the lower energy requirements for the spectrofluorimetric detection compared to the power-intensive HPLC and MS instrumentation also add to the overall green chemistry credentials of the developed approach. All these characteristics highlight the higher AGREE score, a metric based on the 12 principles of green chemistry, for the proposed GQD-based fluorescent sensor with value of 0.79 (Fig. 5 A) compared to 0.61 for HPLC-UV method (Fig. 5B) [7] suggesting its enhanced green attributes. Analytical practicality and applicability of the developed method resemble to great extent to the reported chromatographic method [7] with BAGI scores 75.0 (Figs. 5C) and 77.5 (Fig. 4D), respectively. Such comprehensive evaluation of the greenness, blueness, and overall practicality establishes the developed GQD-based fluorescent sensor as a promising analytical technique for the facile, sensitive, and environment-friendly quantification of abacavir in various matrices.

In summary, this study reports the development of a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent sensor based on GQDs for the determination of the antiretroviral drug, abacavir. Different experimental parameters influencing the fluorescence quenching response were systematically optimized, yielding a linear range of 100–1000 ng/mL with a remarkably low detection limit of 34 ng/mL. Besides, the sensing mechanism was explored using Stern-Volmer kinetics and DFT calculations, providing insights into the adsorption and interaction of abacavir with the GQDs. The method was successfully applied for the quantification of abacavir in pharmaceutical formulations and spiked human plasma samples with excellent recoveries, demonstrating its practical utility. Compared to the traditional HPLC and mass spectrometric methods, the developed GQD-based fluorescent assay offers several advantages in terms of simplicity, rapidity, cost-effectiveness, and environmental compatibility, making it a promising alternative for routine analysis and therapeutic drug monitoring of abacavir.

Future studies can focus on validating the developed method with clinical samples and extending the GQD-based platform for the simultaneous detection of other antiretroviral drugs, which would aid in the therapeutic drug monitoring and optimization of HIV/AIDS treatment regimens. Moreover, investigating the interaction dynamics of abacavir with other commonly prescribed antiretroviral drugs via molecular docking and DFT calculations can provide deeper insights into possible drug-drug interactions and enhance the efficacy of combination therapies in HIV treatment, aligning with the ever-evolving needs of personalized medicine. The integration of such advanced analytical techniques will not only contribute to a better understanding of abacavir's pharmacokinetics but also support the development of tailored therapeutic strategies that optimize treatment outcomes for individuals living with HIV/AIDS, ensuring adherence and minimizing adverse effects.

Ali Alqahtani: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Taha Alqahtani: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Adel Al Fatease: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ahmed A. Almrasy: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
This research was funded by Deanship of Research and Graduate Studies at King Khalid University, Saudi Arabia, Project No. (RGP2/399/45).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Research and Graduate Studies at King Khalid University for funding this work through Large Research Project under grant number RGP2/399/45.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanisms of CD4+ T lymphocyte cell death in human immunodeficiency virus infection and AIDS

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.B. Alimonti, T.B. Ball, K.R. Fowke, Mechanisms of CD4+ T lymphocyte cell death in human immunodeficiency virus infection and AIDS, Journal of general Virology, 84 (2003) 1649-1661.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pathogenesis of HIV infection: what the virus spares is as important as what it destroys

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Grossman, M. Meier-Schellersheim, W.E. Paul, L.J. Picker, Pathogenesis of HIV infection: what the virus spares is as important as what it destroys, Nature medicine, 12 (2006) 289-295.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improving the adherence to antiretroviral therapy, a difficult but essential task for a successful HIV treatment—clinical points of view and practical considerations

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.A. Iacob, D.G. Iacob, G. Jugulete, Improving the adherence to antiretroviral therapy, a difficult but essential task for a successful HIV treatment-clinical points of view and practical considerations, Frontiers in pharmacology, 8 (2017) 831.
